1: These are the terms of the covenant YHWH commanded Moses to make with the Israelites at Moab, in addition to the covenant at Horeb -- two covenants, stacked, each building on the other. As a type of the covenant structure culminating in the new covenant, this verse establishes the pattern of progressive covenantal development. The Mosaic covenant at Horeb is foundational; the Moab covenant adds to it; the prophets announce a new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31) that both fulfills and supersedes. Hebrews 8:6-13 identifies this sequence: the new covenant is the better covenant; the Mosaic is obsolete in the face of what Christ establishes. The covenants are additive and cumulative, pointing toward their fulfillment in Christ.
2: Moses summoned all Israel: you have seen with your own eyes everything YHWH did in Egypt -- covenant renewal grounded in experienced history. As a type of the NT's ground for covenant commitment, this verse establishes the principle that covenant commitment is based on what YHWH has already done, not on speculation. The NT equivalent: we love because he first loved us (1 John 4:19); the remembrance of the Lord's Supper re-grounds covenant commitment in the concrete historical event of the cross. The covenant renewal of Deuteronomy 29 calls Israel to what the Lord's Supper calls the church: remember what YHWH has done, and renew your commitment on that basis.
3: Those great trials, the signs and wonders you saw with your own eyes -- the emphasis on eyewitness testimony as the ground for covenant commitment. As a shadow of the NT's eyewitness grounding, this verse anticipates the Gospel's insistence on eyewitness: that which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked at and our hands have touched -- this we proclaim (1 John 1:1). The covenant community's faith is grounded in what was seen and verified; the NT community's faith is equally grounded in eyewitness testimony to the resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:3-8). The sign-and-wonder history of the Exodus is the type of the sign-and-wonder history of Christ's ministry and resurrection.
4: Yet to this day YHWH has not given you a mind that understands or eyes that see or ears that hear -- the disability of the unregenerate covenant community despite experiencing the Exodus. This is the verse Paul quotes in Romans 11:8: God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear, down to this very day. As a revelation of God, Deuteronomy 29:4 establishes that YHWH himself is the cause of the perceptual inability -- which Paul applies to Israel's rejection of the gospel. The resolution is the Spirit who opens eyes and ears (1 Corinthians 2:14: the natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit; Ezekiel 36:26-27: I will give you a new heart). Christ sends the Spirit to overcome the disability of Deuteronomy 29:4.
5: Forty years I led you through the wilderness -- your clothes did not wear out and your sandals did not wear out. As a shadow of Christ's miraculous provision, the supernatural preservation of clothing and sandals in the wilderness points toward the God who provides for all needs (Matthew 6:25-34: do not be anxious about what you will eat or what you will wear; your heavenly Father knows you need them). The wilderness provision is the OT type of the NT's trust in the Father's provision through Christ, who declared himself the bread of life (John 6:35) and whose disciples experienced clothing preserved, fish caught, and bread multiplied.
6: You ate no bread and drank no wine or other fermented drink -- so that you would know that I am YHWH your God. The deprivation of normal provision was itself the divine pedagogy: knowing YHWH comes through the removal of ordinary provision and the experience of his direct supply. As a shadow of Christ as true bread and true vine, this verse points toward the one who is both the bread from heaven (John 6:32-35) and the true vine whose blood is the new-covenant wine (John 15:1; Matthew 26:27-29). The wilderness deprivation that revealed YHWH as provider is fulfilled in Christ who himself is the provision: I am the bread of life and I am the true vine.
7: When you reached this place, Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan came out to fight and we defeated them. The covenant history includes military victories as evidence of YHWH's faithfulness. As a shadow of Christ's victories over opposing powers, this verse is one of many OT battle-accounts that the NT interprets spiritually: our struggle is not against flesh and blood but against rulers, against authorities, against the powers of this dark world (Ephesians 6:12). The victories over Sihon and Og are the type of Christ's victory over the spiritual rulers that oppose God's people -- through the cross he disarmed principalities and authorities (Colossians 2:15).
8: We took their land and gave it as an inheritance to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. The distribution of conquered land as covenant inheritance points toward the NT's inheritance theology: blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who according to his great mercy caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ, to an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you (1 Peter 1:3-4). The land inheritance of Transjordan is the type of the imperishable inheritance secured by the resurrection.
9: Observe the terms of this covenant so that you may be successful in everything you do. The conditional success-promise that the covenant holds out. As a revelation of God fulfilled in Christ, this verse is the conditional promise that 2 Corinthians 1:20 resolves: all the promises of God find their yes in Christ -- for all the promises of God, he is the yes. The success conditioned on covenant observance is now given unconditionally to those in Christ because he is the covenant-keeper whose faithfulness secures the promise for all who are in him. In Christ, success in everything is not a condition but a gift (Philippians 4:13: I can do all things through him who strengthens me).
10: All of you are standing today before YHWH your God -- leaders, officials, elders, all the men of Israel. The covenant assembly includes the full range of the community's structure. As a shadow of the eschatological assembly before YHWH, this verse anticipates the scene of Revelation 7:9: a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people, and language, standing before the throne and before the Lamb. The covenant assembly in Deuteronomy is a specific, historically bounded community; the eschatological assembly in Revelation is universal and eternal, gathered by Christ from the ends of the earth.
11: Together with your children, wives, and foreigners who live in your camp -- even the woodcutters and water-carriers are included. The covenant assembly is not limited to the religiously significant; the humblest servants are included. As a shadow of the gospel's radical inclusion, this verse anticipates Jesus's table fellowship with tax collectors, sinners, and outsiders (Luke 15:1-2), and the NT's consistent theme that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free (Galatians 3:28). The woodcutter and water-carrier of Deuteronomy's covenant assembly are the type of those who, in the NT, are first at the table of the kingdom.
12: You are here to enter into a covenant with YHWH your God, a covenant he is sealing with an oath today. The covenant-entry is a solemn, oath-bound commitment on both sides. As a type of baptism as covenant-entry, this verse anticipates the moment when the new covenant people enter their covenant with God through baptism: all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death (Romans 6:3). The covenant-entry moment of standing before YHWH and receiving his oath is the type of the baptismal moment when the new covenant community is sealed by the Spirit (Ephesians 1:13-14) and enters into the covenant Christ has secured.
13: To confirm you this day as his people, that he may be your God as he promised and swore to your ancestors. The covenant's purpose is mutual identification: YHWH's people, YHWH as their God. As a direct type of the NT's I-will-be-your-God promise, this verse is the recurring covenant formula (I will be your God and you will be my people, Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 37:27) that reaches its fullest form in Revelation 21:3: God's dwelling place is now among the people and he will dwell with them, they will be his people and God himself will be with them and be their God. Christ is the one through whom this covenant identity is permanently established.
14: I am making this covenant not only with you who stand here today before YHWH our God. The covenant extends beyond those physically present at Moab. As a direct type of the New Covenant's universal reach, this verse is the principle Acts 2:39 applies: the promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off -- for all whom the Lord our God will call. The covenant made in Moab that extended beyond those present is the type of the covenant in Christ's blood that extends to all who will ever be called, in every time and place. The covenant's scope is not determined by geography or generation.
15: But also with those who are not here with us today -- the future recipients of the covenant oath. As a direct type of the New Covenant's inclusion of Gentiles, this verse establishes the principle that the Sinai and Moab covenants were always meant to reach beyond their immediate participants. Paul argues in Galatians 3:8 that when Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, it announced the gospel in advance to Abraham. The covenant that reaches those not yet present is the OT principle of the gospel's reach to all nations through Christ.
16: You remember how we lived in Egypt and how we traveled through the nations on the way here -- the memory of slavery and pagan influence as motivation for covenant fidelity. As a type of the NT's call to remember former bondage, this verse anticipates Paul's therefore remember that at one time you Gentiles in the flesh... were separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world. But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far have been brought near (Ephesians 2:11-13). The memory of Egypt is transformed in the NT into the memory of spiritual bondage from which Christ has freed his people.
17: You saw their detestable images -- the idols of wood, stone, silver, and gold that the nations carry. The exposure to idolatry during the wilderness journey is the temptation to covenant infidelity. As a revelation of God, YHWH takes seriously the seduction of visible idols and warns against their power. The NT's response to idolatry is similarly urgent: little children, keep yourselves from idols (1 John 5:21); flee from idolatry (1 Corinthians 10:14). But the solution is not merely avoidance -- it is the substitution of Christ who is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15), the one who satisfies the human longing that idols exploit.
18: Make sure there is no root among you producing gall and bitterness -- the warning against the corrupting individual within the covenant community. This is the verse Hebrews quotes directly in Deuteronomy 12:15: see to it that no one falls short of the grace of God and that no bitter root grows up to cause trouble and defile many. The bitter root of Deuteronomy 29:18 is applied in Hebrews to the possibility of apostasy and covenant defection within the Christian community. Christ is the one through whom the grace of God flows that prevents bitterness (Romans 5:5: God's love has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit).
19: The one who hears the covenant oath and reassures himself -- I will be safe even though I persist in following the stubbornness of my heart. The self-deceived covenant member who thinks covenant membership protects regardless of heart-condition. As a type of NT false security, this verse is the background for warnings like Hebrews 10:26-29: if we deliberately keep on sinning after we have received the knowledge of the truth, no sacrifice for sins is left... how much more severely do you think a man deserves to be punished who has trampled the Son of God underfoot? The presumption of Deuteronomy 29:19 is the same presumption Hebrews warns against in those who abuse the new covenant.
20: YHWH will never pardon him -- his anger and jealousy will burn against that person, all the curses of this covenant will fall on him, and YHWH will blot out his name from under heaven. The complete covenant rejection of the one who presumes on membership while persisting in covenant-rebellion. As a revelation of God's justice and the NT's sober warnings, this verse is the ground for the severe passages in Hebrews (Deuteronomy 6:4-6; Deuteronomy 10:26-31). The gospel is not cheap grace -- presuming on covenant membership without genuine faith and repentance does not produce immunity. Christ does not shield the deliberately unrepentant from the judgment Deuteronomy 29:20 announces.
21: YHWH will single him out from all the tribes of Israel for disaster, in keeping with all the curses of the covenant written in this book. The individual singled out for covenant judgment within the covenant community. As a type of the NT's individual accountability within the covenant community, this verse is the principle James applies: judgment without mercy will be shown to anyone who has not been merciful (James 2:13). The covenant community as a whole is not judged for one member's defection, but the individual is accountable before YHWH for his own covenant standing. This is resolved by Christ's intercession (Hebrews 7:25: he always lives to intercede for those who come to God through him).
22: Your descendants who follow you and foreigners from distant lands will see the calamities and diseases that YHWH has sent on that land -- the international witness to covenant judgment. As a revelation of God, covenant judgment is public and witnessed: YHWH's discipline of his covenant people is not private but visible to the nations (this is exactly what happened in 70 CE -- the Roman-Jewish war was the international event of the first century). Jesus warned that Jerusalem's destruction would be visible to all (Matthew 24:2; Luke 21:20-24), and the judgment fell publicly and historically in a way the whole world witnessed.
23: The whole land a wasteland of burning sulfur and salt, nothing planted, nothing sprouting, no vegetation -- like the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah. As a type of eschatological judgment, the Sodom-comparison for covenant-breaking Canaan is the measure Jesus applies to cities that reject the gospel: it will be more bearable on the day of judgment for Sodom and Gomorrah than for that city (Matthew 10:15; Deuteronomy 11:23-24). The Sodom comparison escalates in the NT -- cities that have received the greater revelation of Christ and rejected it face greater judgment than the salt-and-sulfur destruction of Sodom. Christ's gospel raises the stakes of covenant decision.
24: All the nations will ask: why has YHWH done this to this land? Why this fierce, burning anger? The nations' question about covenant judgment reflects the public, internationally visible nature of YHWH's disciplinary action. As a revelation of God, YHWH does not hide his judgment -- it is the occasion for the nations to ask the theological question that the covenant community must then answer. The NT application: the judgment of 70 CE generated exactly this question, answered in NT texts that identify covenant-rejection of Christ as the cause. The question of Deuteronomy 29:24 is the evangelistic opening through which the covenant community explains YHWH's purposes to the watching nations.
25: And the answer will be: because this people abandoned the covenant of YHWH and went and served other gods. The covenant-violation is identified as the cause of the judgment. As a type of the NT's diagnosis of human spiritual condition, this verse identifies the root: abandonment of the covenant with YHWH. Paul's analysis in Romans 1:18-25 follows the same structure: they exchanged the truth of God for a lie and worshiped created things rather than the Creator (Romans 1:25). The abandonment of covenant for idols is the universal human movement that Christ reverses: he came to restore the broken covenant and bring the covenant-abandoning back into covenant relationship (2 Corinthians 5:18-20: God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ).
26: They went and worshiped other gods and bowed down to them -- gods they had not known, gods he had not given them. The double alienation: worshipping gods YHWH had not given them. As a revelation of God, YHWH is the God who gives himself to be known -- and the worship of unknown gods is the abandonment of this personal revelation. The NT inverts this: the unknown god that Athens worshipped (Acts 17:23) is not left unknown -- Paul identifies him as the God who has now revealed himself through the man he has designated, the risen Christ. What was unknown is now made known in Christ; the worship of unknown gods is resolved by the gospel that names the name.
27: Therefore YHWH's anger burned against this land and he brought all the curses written in this book. The theological connection between covenant violation and covenant curse: the curses are not arbitrary but the prescribed covenantal consequence of the prescribed covenant violation. As a revelation of God, this verse establishes YHWH's consistency: the covenant is not ambiguous -- the curses are in the book, the violation is identified, and the consequence follows. The NT's justice-theology works from the same structure: the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23) -- a fixed, announced, consistent consequence. What changes in the NT is not the justice of God but the provision of Christ who bears the wages so that others need not.
28: In furious anger and great wrath YHWH uprooted them from their land and thrust them into another land, as it is now. The physical uprooting of the covenant people as the ultimate covenantal action: removal from the land that is the sign of covenant blessing. As a shadow of the church's status as exiles, this verse anticipates the NT's understanding of the church as sojourners and exiles (1 Peter 1:1; Deuteronomy 2:11): the covenant people have been uprooted from their original homeland and are strangers in a foreign place. But Christ transforms the meaning: the uprooting is not judgment but vocation -- sent into the nations as his witnesses (Acts 1:8). The exile of Deuteronomy 29:28 becomes the mission of the church.
29: The secret things belong to YHWH our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children forever. The hidden/revealed distinction: some of YHWH's purposes are beyond human knowledge, but what he has revealed is for the covenant community permanently. This verse is the OT basis for the NT's theology of mystery and revelation. Paul in Colossians 2:3 says that in Christ all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden -- the secret things of YHWH are now disclosed in Christ (Colossians 1:26-27: the mystery hidden for ages and generations but now revealed to his saints, which is Christ in you, the hope of glory). What was hidden in YHWH's secret counsel is now revealed in Christ; the things revealed in Christ belong to the new covenant community forever (Revelation 22:3-4: they will see his face).
